eth_cross_chain_manager.role: Manager contract that forwarded arbitrary calldata via verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx; the attacker's calls carried msg.sender == manager.

eth_cross_chain_data.role: Storage contract whose putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes was reachable through the manager forward; rotating its keeper let the attacker sign arbitrary withdrawals.

lock_proxy_eth.role: Source-chain lock proxy on Ethereum; held WETH / DAI / WBTC / SHIB / FEI before the drain. (Same as manager in this incident — manager itself custodied funds.)

secondary_chains.name: bsc
secondary_chains.note: BSC-side EthCrossChainManager analogue; attacker drained BUSD/BNB/USDC after the keeper rotation propagated.

secondary_chains.name: polygon
secondary_chains.note: Polygon-side bridge that suffered the same drain mechanism.

root_cause_summary.vulnerable_pattern: EthCrossChainManager.verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx forwarded arbitrary (target, calldata) without restricting target to a whitelisted handler. The forwarded call carried msg.sender == manager.
root_cause_summary.verification_bypass: EthCrossChainData.putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes was guarded only by onlyManager(msg.sender == manager). The forward made that check trivially pass, enabling keeper hijack.
affected_logic: Cross-chain proof acceptance (post-rotation it admits any attacker-signed proof)
root_cause_summary.notes: Although classified as V4 (key compromise), the underlying mechanism is a target-whitelist failure (V3 / state desync) — the keeper key was rotated *via* the protocol's own privileged path.

exploit_characteristics.notes: One keeper-rotation tx per chain (ETH/BSC/Polygon) followed by signed withdrawals draining over $611M. Most funds were eventually returned by the attacker.

references.title: BlockSec — The Further Analysis of the Poly Network Attack
